prompt: From 'P ∨ Q' and 'P', can you validly conclude '¬Q'?
options: No — an inclusive 'or' lets both P and Q be true at once
Yes, because only one disjunct can hold
Yes, since P is already true
hint: In logic '∨' is INCLUSIVE: 'P or Q' allows the case where both are true.
explanation: No — this is the fallacy of affirming a disjunct. Logic's '∨' is inclusive, so 'P ∨ Q' is compatible with both P and Q being true; knowing P tells you nothing about Q. Disjunctive Syllogism is the valid pattern: from 'P ∨ Q' and '¬P', conclude Q.
